coredata.dc:title: Hydrophilic silicon nanoparticles as a turn-off and colorimetric fluorescent probe for curcuminoids detection in food samples and cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Qin-Ying
Wang, Ya-Qian
Jiang, Ming
Cui, Yuanyuan
Yu, Xu
Xu, Li
coredata.dc:description: Hydrophilic fluorescent silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) with good pH stability, salt-tolerance and anti-photobleaching were for the first time prepared from hydrophobic 3-glycidoxypropyltrimethoxysilane. Employing SiNPs as the fluorescence probe, selective quantification of curcuminoids based on the quenching effect was realized with a linearity of 0.046–7.4 μg/mL and a limit of detection of 17.6 ng/mL. Moreover, in light of fluorescence redshift of SiNPs corresponding to the elevated concentration of curcuminoids, a fluorescence colorimetric method was established based on only one extra probe, i.e. herein SiNPs. Thus, semi-quantification of curcuminoids (0–14.7 μg/mL) was visualized from blue to yellow color. Both the developed quantitative and semi-quantitative probe were successfully applied to determine curcuminoids in various actual food samples. Furthermore, SiNPs possessed low cytotoxicity and succeeded in intracellular curcuminoids imaging. The proposed SiNPs could be a promising fluorescence probe for multiple applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrophilic silicon nanoparticles
Fluorescence probe
Colorimetric
Curcuminoids
Food samples
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quantitative detection of curcuminoids using SiNPs as the fluorescence probe
Colorimetric detection of curcuminoids using SiNPs as the fluorescence probe
Analysis of curcuminoids in food samples using SiNPs as the fluorescence probe
Fluorescence quantitative detection of curcuminoids using the SiNPs as the probe
Fluorescence colorimetric detection of curcuminoids using the SiNPs as the single probe
Determination of curcuminoids in actual food samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of SiNPs in dark field (Inset: size distribution of SiNPs). (B) FT-IR spectra of SiNPs and GPTMS. XPS spectra of (C) full range, (D) C 1 s, (E) O 1 s and (F) Si 2p peak of SiNPs.
UV–vis absorption (Abs), excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of SiNPs (Inset: the photographs of SiNPs aqueous solution under visible light (left) and UV light of 365 nm (right)).

(A) Selectivity of SiNPs for curcuminoids over other interferents (X) and the coexisting interference of them. The detailed experimental conditions were as described in Section 2.5. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of the SiNPs under λex of 365 nm upon the addition of various concentrations of curcuminoids (from up to bottom: 0, 0.046, 0.092, 0.18, 0.37, 0.74, 1.8, 3.7, 7.4, 9.2, 18.4, 27.6, 36.8 μg/mL) (Inset: the linear plot between fluorescence response of SiNPs (F0/F at λem of 459 nm) and the concentration of curcuminoids (0.046–7.4 μg/mL). (C) The semiquantitative fluorescence colorimetric photograph of SiNPs for 0–14.7 μg/mL curcuminoids under λex of 365 nm.

(A) The normalized UV–vis absorption spectrum of curcuminoids (9.2 μg/mL) and the normalized fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of SiNPs (50 μL/mL). (B) The fluorescence lifetime decay curves of SiNPs (50 μL/mL) in the absence and presence of curcuminoids (7.4 μg/mL) at λex of 365 nm and λem of 459 nm. (C) The absorbance spectra of 9.2 μg/mL curcuminoids in the absence (black line) and presence (green line) of 50 μL/mL SiNPs stock solution. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(A) Bright field image, fluorescence microscopy images of MCF-7 cells incubated with (B) SiNPs (2 μL/mL), (C) curcuminoids (1.8 μg/mL) and (D) SiNPs (2 μL/mL) and curcuminoids (1.8 μg/mL).
Comparison of the detected amount of curcuminoids in food samples between SiNPs-based fluorescence method and HPLC method.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: F0.05, 4, 4 = 6.39; P > 0.05 indicates there is no statistical difference.

head.ce:title: Hydrophilic silicon nanoparticles as a turn-off and colorimetric fluorescent probe for curcuminoids detection in food samples and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430030, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430030, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shimazu China Co. LTD., Shanghai 200233, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shimazu China Co. LTD., Shanghai 200233, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrophilic fluorescent silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) with good pH stability, salt-tolerance and anti-photobleaching were for the first time prepared from hydrophobic 3-glycidoxypropyltrimethoxysilane. Employing SiNPs as the fluorescence probe, selective quantification of curcuminoids based on the quenching effect was realized with a linearity of 0.046–7.4 μg/mL and a limit of detection of 17.6 ng/mL. Moreover, in light of fluorescence redshift of SiNPs corresponding to the elevated concentration of curcuminoids, a fluorescence colorimetric method was established based on only one extra probe, i.e. herein SiNPs. Thus, semi-quantification of curcuminoids (0–14.7 μg/mL) was visualized from blue to yellow color. Both the developed quantitative and semi-quantitative probe were successfully applied to determine curcuminoids in various actual food samples. Furthermore, SiNPs possessed low cytotoxicity and succeeded in intracellular curcuminoids imaging. The proposed SiNPs could be a promising fluorescence probe for multiple applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Curcuminoids are one kind of natural polyphenol compounds usually derived from the rhizome of Zingiberaceae plants, typically from the turmeric (Curcuma longa L.) (Li et al., 2019). As a natural additive for food and cosmetics accredited by the World Health Organization and Food and Drug Administration, curcuminoids are widely used as the spice and natural coloring agent (Patil et al., 2021; Tang et al., 2021). Curcuminoids are mainly composed of three active components, i.e. the largest contributor curcumin and other two analogs (demethoxycurcumin and bisdemethoxycurcumin), which only differ in numbers of methoxy groups on the aromatic rings (Fig. S1) (Suresh et al., 2021). It has been recognized that curcuminoids especially curcumin had broad pharmacological activities, including anti-inflammatory, anticancer and antioxidant (Li et al., 2019; Sharma et al., 2005), as well as cardioprotection and neuroprotection, etc. (Prasad et al., 2014; Xie et al., 2020). Thus, the three curcuminoids commonly together served as the characteristic markers for quality control of the bioactive components-contained samples, such as herbal medicines, dietary supplements and complementary therapeutic products, etc. (Jia et al., 2021; Suresh et al., 2021; Xie et al., 2020). However, specifically, an overdose of curcumin could cause damage to human health, typically the DNA-inactivation successively inducing the decrease of intracellular adenosine triphosphate levels, finally triggering the tissue necrosis (Mirzaei et al., 2017). Therefore, in view of the wide applications of curcuminoids but the uncertain content of daily intake (Xie et al., 2020), it is essential to develop a reliable and convenient method to quantify the curcuminoids in food samples.

So far, various methods, including spectrophotometric methods (UV–vis spectrophotometry and infrared spectroscopy) (Kotra et al., 2019; Suresh et al., 2021), chromatographic methods (thin layer chromatography (TLC), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), liquid chromatography coupled to mass spectrometry (LC-MS) and capillary electrophoresis (CE)) (Degot et al., 2021; Jia et al., 2021; Kotra et al., 2019; Núñez et al., 2020; Osorio-Tobón et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2018), nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) (Praveen et al., 2021) and electrochemistry (Zokhtareh & Rahimnejad, 2018) etc., were developed to detect curcuminoids. Among them, limited sensitivity (such as UV–vis, TLC and HPLC methods), or expensive instruments along with tedious sample pretreatment or long analysis time (such as HPLC, LC-MS, CE and NMR methods) could be the disadvantages for the sensitive and fast detection of curcuminoids with low cost and less labor involvement. With the properties of high sensitivity and operational convenience, spectrofluormetry using the specific fluorescence probe could be a satisfactory choice to reach the goal (Kotra et al., 2019). Recently, the emerging advancement of fluorescence nanoprobes promoted the related researches. For examples, various elements (N, S, P, Cl, B, etc.) doped fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were proposed and attempted for curcumin detection based on the quenching effect (Bian et al., 2018; Hao et al., 2021; Hu et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2018; Yan et al., 2019). Mn-doped ZnS semiconductor quantum dots were adopted to detect curcumin based on the ratiometric mode (Zhao et al., 2015). Despite of the above effective fluorescence sensors, a fluorescence probe with feasible availability, multiple detection modes, good resistance to actual sample matrix effect and low toxicity was still attractive and necessary for curcuminoids detection, especially with the ability for fast on-site visual detection.

Nowadays, silicon-based fluorescence nanomaterials (fluorescent silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) or silicon quantum dots) have attracted tremendous attention in the area of fluorescence sensing and imaging, for their excellent properties such as low toxicity, high photostability, favorable biocompatibility and biodegradability, low cost and eco-friendliness (Li & Xu, 2020; Ma et al., 2016). However, up to now, the commonly reported fluorescent SiNPs were mostly obtained via amino-containing silanes, e.g. 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane or other silanes containing multi-nitrogen atoms (Chen et al., 2013; Han et al., 2018; Ma et al., 2016). Although these amino-containing silanes yielded SiNPs with relatively high fluorescence quantum yield, the limitation was apparent with the restricted silicon source and thus the subsequent applications (Chen et al., 2013). Another possible reason for this phenomenon may be due to the hydrophobicity of the most non-amino silanes, which in most cases resulted in hydrophobic SiNPs, thus severely limited applications (Liu et al., 2018). Therefore, exploring more silane sources for SiNPs would facilitate the related technology. Inspired by the previous work and the reverse thinking, we tried to use the hydrophobic silane sources to construct the hydrophilic fluorescent SiNPs.

In the present study, the hydrophilic fluorescent SiNPs were successfully prepared by using hydrophobic 3-glycidoxypropyltrimethoxysilane (GPTMS) as silicon source via a simple hydrothermal method for the first time. The modulation of hydrophilicity of the SiNPs was realized by using appropriate reacting media, herein water. Curcuminoids were found to quench the fluorescence of SiNPs accompanying with the redshift of the maximum fluorescence emission peak. Thus, the proposed SiNPs were utilized as the fluorescence probe for curcuminoids detection in multiple food samples with excellent anti-interference ability, satisfactory sensitivity and high selectivity, which were validated by HPLC method. Additionally, a semiquantitative fluorescence colorimetric method based on the redshift phenomenon was established to fast visually determine curcuminoids in food samples with free of large-scale instrument. Furthermore, the SiNPs showed low cytotoxicity and succeeded in imaging of intracellular curcuminoids, indicating its potential for biological applications. Overall, via a simple and low-cost preparation process instead of complex sensor construction, our new proposed fluorescence SiNPs would simultaneously realize the sensitive turn-off and on-site visual colorimetric dual modes for curcuminoids detection, which could be a promising probe for curcuminoids analysis in both food and biological samples.

Curcumin (≥95%), KCl, CaCl2, BaCl2, MgSO4, ZnCl2, FeCl2·4H2O, FeCl3, CuCl2, CoCl2·6H2O, MnSO4·H2O, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, cysteine, alanine, histidine, phenylalanine, acetyl-phenylalanine, lysine, tryptophan, ascorbic acid, galactose, cholesterol, aspirin and 4-hydroxybenzoic acid were purchased from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Methionine, dopamine, glutathione (GSH), bovine serum albumin (BSA) and ferulic acid were purchased from the Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Cianidanol, rhein, chrysophanol, gallic acid, chlorogenic acid and caffeic acid were obtained from Beijing Wanjia Shouhua Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). GPTMS was obtained from the WD Silicone Co., Ltd (Wuhan, China). Ultrapure water was produced by a Heal Fore NW system (Shanghai, China).

The hydrophilic fluorescent SiNPs were prepared by a hydrothermal method using GPTMS as silicon source and water as the dispersing solvent. Typically, 5.6 mL ultrapure water and 4.4 mL GPTMS (ultimate concentration was 2 M) were mixed in a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After cooling down to the room temperature, the obtained yellow solution, i.e. the SiNPs stock solution, was kept in the dark for further application.

The UV absorption and fluorescence spectra of SiNPs were measured by a Lambda 365 UV–vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA) and an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), respectively. The transmission electron microscopic (TEM) image was captured by a Talos F200X electron microscope (FEI, Netherlands). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic (FT-IR) analysis was conducted by an AVATAR 360 spectrometer (Thermo, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurements were performed with an AXIS Supra by Kratos Analytical Inc. using monochromatized Al Kα radiation (hv = 1486.69 eV, 225 W) as X-ray source with a base pressure of 10−9 torr. The fluorescence lifetime decay curves were recorded by a Fluorescence Lifetime Spectrometer with Felix 32 system (Photon Technology International, Canada).

The fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) of SiNPs (QYSiNP) were measured with quinine sulfate as the reference (QYr is 54% at 360 nm in 0.1 M H2SO4). Briefly, the SiNPs and quinine sulfate were diluted to six different concentrations with ultrapure water and 0.1 M H2SO4 solution, respectively, ensuring the absorbance value of each solution at 360 nm below 0.1. The QYSiNP was calculated according to Eq. (1),
(1)
Q
Y
SiNPs
=
Q
Y r
k s
η s 2
/
k r
η r 2
where the subscript s and r represent SiNPs and quinine sulfate, respectively; k denotes the slope of fitted linear curve of integrated fluorescence area versus absorbance value; ƞ is the refractive index of solvent (ƞ of ultrapure water and 0.1 M H2SO4 solution are both 1.33) (Li et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection of curcuminoids using SiNPs as the fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: For curcuminoids is insoluble in cold water, curcuminoids stock solution (368.4 μg/mL) was herein prepared in ethanol and further diluted using ultrapure water as working solution. In a typical detection process, 100 μL SiNPs stock solution was mixed with 2 mL curcuminoids aqueous solution of specific concentration (0–36.8 μg/mL). Afterwards, the solution was for the fluorescence measurement at the excitation wavelength (λex) of 365 nm and emission wavelength (λem) of 459 nm with both excitation and emission slit widths of 10 nm. The fluorescence response (F0/F) was then calculated, where F0 and F denoted the fluorescence intensity of SiNPs in the absence and presence of curcuminoids, respectively. To optimize the detection conditions, pH value of 2–12, the amount of added SiNPs in terms of 50–500 μL and the incubation time of 0.5–30 min were investigated.

For selectivity experiment, different cations (e.g. K+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Cr2+ and Ni2+) of 25 μM, amino acids (e.g. cysteine, alanine, methionine, histidine, phenylalanine, acetyl-phenylalanine, lysine and tryptophan) of 10 μg/mL, biological endogenous molecules and drugs (e.g. dopamine, ascorbic acid, GSH, galactose, cholesterol, BSA and aspirin) of 10 μg/mL as well as polyphenols (4-hydroxybenzoic acid, ferulic acid, cianidanol, rhein, chrysophanol, gallic acid, chlorogenic acid, caffeic acid) of 10 μg/mL were chosen to assess their impacts on fluorescence intensity of SiNPs. Each selected substance (2 mL) of the specific concentration was mixed with 100 μL of SiNPs stock solution and then used for the fluorescence measurement as above. To evaluate the coexisting effect, 100 μL SiNPs was added into 2 mL mixed solution of 3.7 μg/mL curcuminoids and the individual interferent of the specified concentrations as indicated above, and then for fluorescence measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of curcuminoids using SiNPs as the fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: For fluorescence colorimetric detection of curcuminoids, a semiquantitative fluorescence colorimetric card was constructed by dispersing 100 μL SiNPs stock solution to 2 mL curcuminoids aqueous solution of respective concentration (0–14.7 μg/mL), and photographed at λex of 365 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of curcuminoids in food samples using SiNPs as the fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: For food samples analysis, ten actual samples containing curcuminoids (i.e. three different turmeric powder samples, three different curry powder samples, ginger powder, mustard sauce, turmeric solid drink and sobering turmeric granule purchased from the local market or online) were chosen for quantitative analysis using SiNPs as the fluorescence probe. The pretreatment of these food samples referred to the previous work (Hu et al., 2019). Briefly, 0.1 g powder or sauce sample was weighted and sonicated with the addition of 1 mL ethanol. The granule sample was first ground into powder and then treated in the same manner. After sonicating for 15 min, the mixture was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was then collected and filtrated through a 0.45 μm syringe filter, and the obtained filtered extract was diluted appropriately with ultrapure water to meet the linear detection range of SiNPs for curcuminoids. Then 100 μL SiNPs stock solution was added into 2 mL diluted extract solution and the fluorescence intensity at λex/λem of 365/459 nm was recorded. The calibration curve developed in Section 2.5 was used to calculate the concentration of curcuminoids. Besides, recovery test was conducted by spiking with curcuminoids of three different concentrations (0.74, 1.84, 5.53 μg/mL) in the diluted specific food sample extracts as listed in Table S2. Particularly, three polyphenols-rich samples, i.e. kiwifruit peel, apple and tomato, which were detected by the HPLC method to be free of target curcuminoids (data not shown), were involved. The recovery (%) was calculated by dividing the sum of the original and added concentrations of curcuminoids by the measured concentration of curcuminoids.

To validate the accuracy of SiNPs-based fluorescence method for curcuminoids quantification in food samples, HPLC analysis was performed on a Dionex Ultimate 3000 chromatographic system (California, USA), which included an eluent degassing device, two Ultimate 3000 pumps, an Ultimate 3000 automated injector, an Ultimate 3000 RS column compartment, and a diode array detector. The Chromeleon software was used for data acquisition and analysis. The food sample pretreatment was the same as above but with different dilution multiple to meet the linearity range of HPLC analysis. The separation was performed on an ODS column (2.1 mm × 150 mm, 3 μm) at room temperature. The mobile phase was composed of acetonitrile and 5% HAcO aqueous solution (65:35, v/v) with a flow rate of 0.2 mL/min. The detection wavelength was fixed at 420 nm for curcuminoids quantification and the injection volume was 5 μL. At last, the results measured by SiNPs-based fluorescence method and HPLC method were statistically analyzed by one-way ANOVA in IBM SPSS Statistics 25 (International Business Machines Corporation, USA) to verify the accuracy of the proposed method.

As for the semi-quantitative fluorescence colorimetric detection, the above extracts of actual food samples were first diluted specific times with water as indicated in Fig. S6. 100 μL SiNPs stock solution was then added into 2 mL respective diluted extracts. The semi-quantitation was conducted by using the colorimetric card as a reference which was established in Section 2.6.

MCF-7 cell was chosen to evaluate the cytotoxicity of SiNPs in vitro by MTT assay. Briefly, 100 μL MCF-7 cells (5 × 104/mL in DMEM medium) were seeded and cultured in the 96-well plate for 24 h. Then the culture medium was discarded and the cells were incubated in the fresh DMEM medium containing SiNPs of different concentrations (0, 0.8, 1.6, 2, 21, 42, 84 μL/mL) for another 24 h. Afterwards, 10 μL MTT (5 mg/mL in PBS) was added into each well to incubate for 4 h. At last, 100 μL DMSO was added and the absorbance at 490 nm of each well was recorded using a microplate reader (Biorad, USA). The cell viability was calculated by the Eq. (2),
(2)
Cell
viability
( % )
=
A
test
/
A
blank
× 100 %
where Atest and Ablank represent the absorbance of cells incubated in the presence and absence of SiNPs, respectively.

The MCF-7 cells were seeded in a 12-well plate and cultured in DMEM at 37 °C and 5% CO2 atmosphere for 24 h. Then the culture medium was discarded and the cells were incubated in the fresh DMEM medium containing 2 μL/mL SiNPs for another 24 h. The cells were rinsed with PBS for three times to remove the free SiNPs, and observed by a fluorescence microscopy (UOP, DSY5000X, China).

As for imaging of intracellular curcuminoids, MCF-7 cells were firstly pre-incubated with 1.8 μg/mL curcuminoids for 4 h. After the culture medium was discarded, the curcuminoids-contained cells were incubated with SiNPs-contained (2 μL/mL) fresh DMEM medium for 24 h. The cells were then rinsed with PBS for three times and observed by the fluorescence microscopy.

The hydrophilic fluorescent SiNPs were prepared by using hydrophobic GPTMS as silicon source and water as the solvent via a simple hydrothermal method. During the hydrothermal process with high temperature and high pressure, the epoxy group of GPTMS might react with water much easily and generate hydrophilic hydroxyl groups (Embarek & Sahli, 2020), and the resultant SiNPs were thus hydrophilic.

During synthesis, the concentration of GPTMS, hydrothermal temperature and time which were supposed to influence the physicochemical property, especially the fluorescence property of SiNPs were investigated. With the elevated concentration of GPTMS, the QYSiNP gradually increased and then decreased (Fig. S2A), while the obtained SiNPs solution became more and more sticky at high GPTMS concentrations (3 M and 4 M), which may be inconvenient in applications. Thus, as a compromise, 2 M GPTMS was adopted for further optimization. Fig. S2B indicated that higher hydrothermal temperature was more favorable for enhancing the QYSiNP during the tested temperature of 140 °C to 200 °C. With the prolonging reaction time, the QYSiNP gradually increased until 8 h and then reached a plateau at 10 h (Fig. S2C). Therefore, the optimum preparation condition of SiNPs was as described in Section 2.2, and thus-obtained fluorescent SiNPs possessed the QY of 5.1%.

The morphology of SiNPs was observed by TEM as imaged in Fig. 1 A. The SiNPs were quasi spheres in shape. Based on the statistical results of 85 SiNPs randomly selected, the size distribution of SiNPs ranged from 8.7 nm to 40.0 nm with an average diameter of 20.0 nm. FT-IR analysis was used to characterize the functional groups of SiNPs as revealed in Fig. 1B. The absorption peaks were identified as νO -H 3410 cm−1; νas C-H 2931 cm−1, νs C-H 2877 cm−1 and δC-H 1454 cm−1; νas C-O-C 1202 cm−1, νSi-O-C 1120 cm−1, νSi-O-Si 1048 cm−1 and νs Si-O 783 cm−1; as well as the typical absorption of epoxy group at 915 cm−1 (Dong et al., 2017; Ghodsinia & Akhlaghinia, 2019; Han et al., 2018; Ma et al., 2016). By contrast with the FT-IR spectrum of hydrophobic silicon source of GPTMS (Fig. 1B), the appearance of OH in SiNPs could be responsible for the hydrophilicity of these SiNPs. Besides, the appearance of νSi-O-Si peak indicated the occurrence of the hydrolytic condensation reaction of GPTMS during the synthesis of SiNPs. On the other hand, the sharp peak at 822 cm−1 assigning to νas C-O in the epoxy group of GPTMS disappeared in the spectrum of SiNPs, which suggested that most epoxy groups could be opened during the formation of SiNPs. Furthermore, XPS was employed to identify the valence state of elements and surface chemical composition of the SiNPs. In Fig. 1C, the full scan spectrum of SiNPs displayed three typical peaks at 102.45 eV, 286.35 eV and 532.55 eV, corresponding to Si 2p (8.7%), C 1 s (56.0%) and O 1 s (35.3%), respectively. The high-resolution C 1 s analysis (Fig. 1D) revealed the presence of C-Si (283.96 eV), CC (284.74 eV) and CO (286.37 eV) (Liu et al., 2018). The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1E) composed of two subpeaks at 532.42 eV and 532.99 eV, denoting COC/C-OH and Si-O groups, respectively (Han et al., 2018). Two dominant peaks at 102.2 eV and 102.7 eV in Si 2p spectrum (Fig. 1F) were ascribed to Si-C and Si-O groups, respectively (Liu et al., 2018). It can be seen that XPS results coincided well with the FT-IR results, indicating that epoxy groups of GPTMS would be opened to generate –OH, resulting in the hydrophilicity of SiNPs.

Both UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of SiNPs are depicted in Fig. 2 . The SiNPs displayed a wide absorption band ranging from 200 nm to 400 nm with a plateau at 220 nm. The end absorption of SiNPs could be attributed to the n-δ* transition of heteroatom (i.e. O in SiNPs) (Zhang et al., 2018). Fig. 2 showed both the emission and excitation bands of SiNPs were broad, which also well illustrated the transparent pale yellow of the SiNPs aqueous solution under visible light and blue fluorescence under UV irradiation at 365 nm (inset of Fig. 2). The broad fluorescence band could be attributed to the multiple energy level transitions resulting from polydisperse fluorescence centers of SiNPs, owing to their different sizes or surface energy traps (Xia et al., 2019). The excitation dependent photoluminescence phenomenon of SiNPs was observed (Fig. S3A and B). Corresponding to the gradually increasing λex from 305 nm to 455 nm, the maximum λem (
λ
em
max
) red shifted from 365 nm to 525 nm.

To further investigate the optical properties of SiNPs, the fluorescence stability of SiNPs was studied under various conditions. As exhibited in Fig. S4A and B, the fluorescence intensity of SiNPs remained almost stable during a wide pH range from 3 to 10 as well as under the high ionic strength with NaCl concentration up to 1 M. However, the SiNPs showed lessened fluorescence intensity by ~25% at pH 2 and ~10%–20% at pH > 10. Predictably, in the strong acidic media (pH 2), the breakage of siloxane bonds on the SiNPs might occur (Claessens & van Straten, 2004), inducing the decrease of emission sites of SiNPs thus weakening their fluorescence intensity. Though the SiNPs had higher alkaline tolerance up to pH 10 comparing with conventional bare silica, the dissolution of Si-O-Si linkage of SiNPs at pH 11–12 may still take place (Kirkland et al., 1995), leading to the weakened fluorescence intensity. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of SiNPs remained relatively constant under continuous UV irradiation at 365 nm for 90 min (Fig. S4C), and >80% of fluorescence intensity still remained under the natural light exposure up to 60 days (Fig. S4D). The above results revealed that the SiNPs possessed satisfactory pH-stability, salt-tolerance and anti-photobleaching, which exhibited their merits for further applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quantitative detection of curcuminoids using the SiNPs as the probe
ce:section.ce:para: In our preliminary study, the fluorescence of SiNPs was found to be quenched accompanying with the redshift of λem by curcuminoids. Hence, SiNPs were investigated as the fluorescence probe for curcuminoids detection. In order to obtain the highest sensitivity for curcuminoids detection by SiNPs, the detection conditions including the pH value, the amount of SiNPs and the incubation time were firstly optimized using the F0/F as the criterion. As depicted in Fig. S5A, the response of SiNPs for curcuminoids remained relatively invariant at pH 2–10. Though the F0/F reached the maximum at pH 12, both SiNPs and curcuminoids may be unstable at the strong alkaline condition (Sharma et al., 2005). Thus, from the perspective of operational feasibility and simplicity, the detection pH was unadjusted. In addition, Fig. S5B indicated that the amount of SiNPs in terms of 50–500 μL had less significant effect on respond signal, and the slight larger F0/F value was achieved with the addition of 100 μL SiNPs, which was chosen as the optimum. From Fig. S5C, it can be seen that the response of SiNPs for curcuminoids was very rapid and immediately reached almost steady F0/F value once SiNPs and the target analyte were mixed. Thus, the incubation time effect could be ignored, with no need for deliberately controlling the incubation time in terms of the flexibility of detection. In sum, the optimized conditions for detection of curcuminoids were as described in Section 2.5.

In order to estimate the actual application potential of the SiNPs probe for curcuminoids detection, the selectivity test was carried out as described in Section 2.5. Negligible fluorescence response (in Fig. 3 A) and no redshift of λem (data not shown) was observed when each of these interferents (cations, amino acids, biological endogenous substances and polyphenols) was individually incubated with the SiNPs, indicating the high selectivity of the SiNPs probe towards curcuminoids over other potential interfering species. Moreover, the coexisting test indicated that these interferents exhibited less influence on the detection system for curcuminoids, which demonstrated the stable output of fluorescence response of SiNPs for curcuminoids.

Under the optimal detection condition, the detectability of the SiNPs probe towards curcuminoids was evaluated in water. The fluorescence intensity of SiNPs gradually declined along with the obvious redshift of the
λ
em
max
corresponding to the elevated concentration of curcuminoids from 0 to 36.8 μg/mL (Fig. 3B). However, to ensure the high sensitivity of the detection, the fluorescence response (F0/F) for quantification was calculated at the
λ
em
max
of SiNPs (459 nm). A good linear relationship (y = 0.3169x + 0.9564) with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9907 was obtained between the F0/F and curcuminoids concentration within a linear range of 0.046–7.4 μg/mL (inset of Fig. 3B). The limit of detection (LOD) for curcuminoids was calculated by 3σ/k to be 17.6 ng/mL, where σ was the standard deviation of the blank SiNPs solution and k was the slope of the calibration curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence colorimetric detection of curcuminoids using the SiNPs as the single probe
ce:section.ce:para: As demonstrated in Fig. 3B, the fluorescence of SiNPs transitioned from blue fluorescence to yellow fluorescence with an obvious redshift of λem corresponding to the enhanced concentrations of curcuminoids. Based on this phenomenon, a fluorescence colorimetric method was established for fast visual semi-quantitation of curcuminoids. Within 0–14.7 μg/mL as photographed in Fig. 3C, the fluorescence color change from blue to yellow color of SiNPs with the elevated concentration of curcuminoids was recognizable, while nearly no fluorescence was observed for curcuminoids itself in aqueous solution (data not shown). Therefore, by employing the only single probe SiNPs without complex construction, visual fluorescence colorimetry was established for semi-quantitation of curcuminoids, which could serve as a rapid and convenient approach to detect curcuminoids by naked eyes.

The comparison between the proposed SiNPs fluorescence probe and the reported fluorescence sensors for the major ingredient of curcuminoids (curcumin) detection is listed in Table S1. In contrast to the single detection mode (turn-off) for curcumin detection in aqueous solution by the reported various fluorescence CDs, the proposed fluorescent SiNPs probe was characteristic of the ability for dual detection modes, i.e. fluorescence turn-off and colorimetric, which served as the quantitative and semiquantitative approaches for curcuminoids detection, respectively. The specific fluorescence color change and rapid response for curcuminoids endowed the SiNPs probe with the ability of fast on-site visual monitoring of curcuminoids by naked eyes. Besides, compared with the attempt to construct composite fluorescence sensor or employ alternative detection matrix (i.e. organic solvent) to reach the same goal (Table S1), the proposed SiNPs possessed the merits of a simpler, more feasible and lower-cost preparation method, as well as a more convenient and environment-friendly detection process. The developed method based on SiNPs as the probe exhibited comparable or wider linear range and satisfactory LOD (17.6 ng/mL) compared with other fluorescence sensors. Moreover, by comparison with the common used HPLC method, our developed SiNPs-based fluorescence method owned higher sensitivity (Osorio-Tobón et al., 2016), faster detection and economic costs, with the excellent applicability in actual food samples as discussed later (Section 3.6). Furthermore, the fluorescence colorimetric detection method based on the single SiNPs probe enabled the free of the dependence of expensive instruments, which could realize fast and convenient on-site detection, confirming the flexibility of the SiNPs probe. However, the fluorescence method could not achieve the purpose of separation, thus, the analysis of specific curcuminoids analogs might still fall back on the chromatographic method.

The possible detection mechanism of the SiNPs sensor for curcuminoids was investigated in detail. As displayed in Fig. 4A, a wide overlap between the absorbance band of curcuminoids and the excitation and emission bands of SiNPs was obviously observed, indicating the possible fluorescence quenching mechanism, i.e. inner filter effect (IFE) (Hu et al., 2019). Specifically, curcuminoids could shield the excitation light for SiNPs, but absorb the emitted fluorescence from SiNPs (Chen et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2019), thus causing the decrease of fluorescence emission intensity of SiNPs. As another demonstration that the average lifetimes of SiNPs in the absence and presence of curcuminoids were the same to each other as measured to be 8.36 ns and 8.14 ns, respectively (Fig. 4 B). Thus, no significant electron-transfer process between SiNPs and curcuminoids existed, further indicating the fluorescence quenching mechanism of IFE (Liu et al., 2018). Moreover, Fig. 4C revealed that the absorbance intensity of curcuminoids less than 478 nm was further enhanced in the presence of the probe SiNPs, indicating that the quenching effect was further magnified in the detection system based on IFE (Zhang et al., 2019), which could be responsible for its excellent sensitivity.

In detail, the overlap between the absorbance band of curcuminoids and the emission band of SiNPs mainly located on the blue fluorescence zone (Fig. 4A), which meant that curcuminoids may mainly absorb the emitted blue fluorescence of SiNPs. Moreover, the enhanced and narrowed absorbance band of curcuminoids-SiNPs system (Fig. 4C) could further enlarge the overlap proportion on the blue fluorescence zone while reduced the overlap proportion on the green-yellow fluorescence zone (λem > 500 nm), thus weakening the quenching effect of curcuminoids for SiNPs on green-yellow fluorescence zone. Therefore, a redshift of the
λ
em
max
of SiNPs occurred as depicted in Fig. 3B. Hence, in light of the absorption property of the target analyte as well as the specific interaction between the SiNPs and analyte, the SiNPs could realize the colorimetric detection of curcuminoids from blue to yellow fluorescence based on the single fluorescence probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of curcuminoids in actual food samples
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the actual applicability of the proposed SiNPs probe, curcuminoids determination in ten food samples were conducted, as listed in Table 1 . The developed SiNPs sensor possessed satisfactory selectivity, and the studied food matrix almost had no effect on the detection. Thus, the calibration curve established in Section 3.3 could be directly used for quantification of curcuminoids in these complex food sample extracts. In addition, to verify the accuracy of the developed SiNPs-based fluorescence method, HPLC method was performed. The chromatographic analysis result revealed the possible co-existence of three curcuminoids (curcumin, demethoxycurcumin and bisdemethoxycurcumin) in the tested curcumin standard (Fig. S6A) (Jayaprakasha et al., 2002; Patil et al., 2021; Syed et al., 2015). As referred to previous works (Patil & Rathod, 2020; Patil et al., 2021; Suresh et al., 2021), a linear relationship between the three peak area and the concentration of total curcuminoids was obtained by the HPLC method (y = 0.8337x-1.8801, R2 = 0.9981) within a linear range of 3.7–36.8 μg/mL. As compared in Table 1, the measured amount of curcuminoids by the SiNPs sensor coincided well with the HPLC result. The one-way ANOVA results indicated that there was no significant difference of the precision (F < F0.05, 4, 4 = 6.39) and accuracy (P > 0.05) of the proposed method (95% confidence level) comparing with HPLC method. Besides, the results of recovery rest of the ten curcuminoids-containing food samples with satisfactory recovery (99.43–104.08%) and relative standard deviation (RSD, 0.41–2.81%) further demonstrated the good accuracy and precision of the proposed SiNPs-based fluorescence method (Table S2). Moreover, SiNPs exhibited excellent specificity for the spiked curcuminoids among three polyphenols-rich food extract, i.e. kiwifruit peel, apple and tomato, with good recovery of 99.84–102.27% and RSD of 0.18–1.55%. Above results indicated that there was little matrix interference in the tested food samples, as well as little response difference of SiNPs towards the three possible different curcuminoid ingredients. It could be speculated that the methoxy group of curcumin may be not the effective binding site with SiNPs, thus the SiNPs could not discriminate the different curcuminoids. Overall, the proposed SiNPs could accurately measure the total active ingredients of curcumin, i.e. curcuminoids.

Moreover, the semiquantitative fluorescence colorimetric card developed in Section 3.4 was utilized to fast estimate the amount of curcuminoids in food samples (Fig. S6B). The fluorescence redshift of SiNPs while detecting curcuminoids would ensure its specificity in actual applications. It can be seen that the semiquantitative results agreed well with the quantitative results in Table 1, which was faster and more convenient to preliminarily on-site estimate the concentration of curcuminoids in the actual food samples by naked eyes.

MTT assay result revealed that the average viability of MCF-7 cell was >90% at the dosage of SiNPs up to 42 μL/mL and >84% at 84 μL/mL (Fig. S7), demonstrating the low toxicity and favorable biocompatibility of SiNPs. Furthermore, the SiNPs were attempted as the fluorescence probe for cell imaging as photographed in Fig. 5 . The MCF-7 cells showed blue fluorescence at λex of 400–440 nm in which the cell morphology coincided with the bright field image, indicating the SiNPs could realize the whole cell imaging. More importantly, once SiNPs were incubated with the MCF-7 cells pretreated with curcuminoids, a redshift from blue to green fluorescence turned on for the studied cells, while no fluorescence could be observed on only curcuminoids pretreated cells, which indicated the ability of SiNPs for imaging the curcuminoids in cells. Thus, it was promising for SiNPs to visually monitor intracellular curcuminoids based on the fluorescence color change.

Hydrophilic fluorescent silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) were prepared for the first time by using hydrophobic 3-glycidoxypropyltrimethoxysilane (GPTMS) as silane source and water as reacting solvent through a simple hydrothermal method. As a complimentary to the conventional amino-containing silane source, the hydrophobic GPTMS could generate hydrophilic fluorescent SiNPs in a proper way. Curcuminoids was found to selectively quench the fluorescence of the proposed SiNPs, and thus the SiNPs were used as fluorescence probe to determine curcuminoids based on the turn-off fluorescence. The quantitative linearity range of 0.046–7.4 μg/mL with a limit of detection of 17.6 ng/mL was obtained. Moreover, corresponding to the increasing concentration of curcuminoids, the redshift of the fluorescence of SiNPs simultaneously occurred. Hence, this phenomenon was used to establish the fluorescence colorimetric semi-quantitation of curcuminoids by using the only single SiNPs as the probe. The fluorescence color of the colorimetric system varied from blue to yellow corresponding to curcuminoids concentration of 0–14.7 μg/mL. Accurate detection of curcuminoids in ten food samples based on the fluorescence turn-off response as well as semi-quantitation based on the constructed colorimetric card was achieved, which was verified by HPLC method. Moreover, with the merit of low cytotoxicity, SiNPs was successfully used in cell imaging and intracellular curcuminoids visual monitoring. In summary, the proposed SiNPs could serve as a promising fluorescence probe to detect and monitor curcuminoids in food and biological samples with dual modes.

Qin-Ying Li: Methodology, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Ya-Qian Wang: Methodology. Ming Jiang: Writing - original draft. Yuanyuan Cui: . Xu Yu: Visualization, Writing - original draft, Funding acquisition. Li Xu: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support of this research by the Program for HUST Academic Frontier Youth Team (No. 2019QYTD09), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2020kfyXJJS082) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21804105). The authors also thank the Analytical and Testing Center of HUST for the material characterization.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.130629.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
